summary: Reviewed input.ts, evidence.ts, task.ts for stdin-only refactor. No critical or high findings. Stdin read is unbounded (fs.readFileSync(0)) but CLI runs with caller's privileges, no trust boundary crossed — LOW DoS. JSON parse errors surface only V8 parser message + computed line/column, never raw stdin content — no secret leakage. parseSetFlags uses Object.create(null) and DANGEROUS_KEYS denylist on both full key and array-prefix key. Stdin overlay via Object.assign onto JSON.parse result is safe (own-prop __proto__, not prototype mutator). Windows EAGAIN/EOF/UV_EOF normalized; unknown errors re-framed with code preserved, no masking. No new path sinks.
concerns: LOW: fs.readFileSync(0) in readInputData has no size cap. Local-only DoS — an agent or user piping a multi-GB payload could OOM the CLI. Not exploitable across a trust boundary since caller already has process privileges. Consider a soft cap (e.g. 16 MiB) if adversarial local input ever becomes in-scope.

LOW: parseSetFlags filters __proto__/constructor/prototype keys, but readInputData does not scrub the same keys from stdin JSON before passing data to schema validation and Object.assign merge. Not exploitable (JSON.parse treats __proto__ as an own property, not a prototype setter), but the hygiene is inconsistent between the two input paths.
